text.title: Laravel Patterns Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common agent mistakes: Eloquent in controller, bypassed FormRequest, hidden exceptions, PHP version mismatch.

antipattern.description: Eloquent::find() or User::create() called directly inside the controller despite the service-layer prompt.
antipattern.reason: LLMs frequently fall back to Active Record in controllers because it is the shortest path. Code review must explicitly verify that controllers contain no Eloquent calls.

antipattern.description: Validation duplicated in both FormRequest and Service: two sources of truth for the same rules drift apart over time.
antipattern.reason: FormRequest is the single validation boundary; services receive only validated() data and trust it. Duplicating rules in the service creates divergence when one is updated.

antipattern.description: try/catch wrapping DB::transaction that swallows the exception and returns a success response.
antipattern.reason: Swallowing the exception prevents the transaction from rolling back semantically (it does physically, but the caller believes it succeeded). Let exceptions propagate to the framework exception handler.

antipattern.description: private readonly constructor promotion on PHP < 8.1 targets: syntax error at runtime.
antipattern.reason: readonly property promotion requires PHP 8.1+. Agents must read composer.json "require.php" constraint before emitting this syntax.

antipattern.description: Repository added for every resource without justification, including simple CRUD with a single Eloquent data source.
antipattern.reason: Eloquent is already an Active Record pattern; wrapping it in a repository adds two files and a service provider binding for a single implementation with no abstraction benefit.

antipattern.description: Missing return type on DB::transaction closure: agent emits mixed return type, breaking PHPStan strict mode analysis.
antipattern.reason: DB::transaction infers the return type from the closure; without an explicit return type hint on the closure, static analysis treats the result as mixed and blocks further type checking.
